# Course policies. {#sec-policies}

This chapter collects the official rules that students need to follow during the semester. Other chapters may summarize or link to these rules, but these policy blocks are the main reference.

<a id="sec-ap"></a>

::: {.callout-important}
### Activities policies.

- Schedule. Exams follow the course calendar (@sec-schedule) and the university’s official dates.
- Duration. $E_1$ and $E_2$ are 1.5-hour in-class activities; $E_F$ lasts 2 hours.
- Homework deadlines. Homework assignments are due at 10:00am on the dates specified in the course calendar (@sec-schedule).
- Policies. Rules on language, group work, late submissions, submission format/location, question requirements, and review-session attendance are defined in [Activities policies](#sec-ap).

1. Exams and homework assignments must be submitted in English. If a submission is not in English, at least 50\% of the overall mark may be deducted.
1. Exams and homework assignments are completed by your assigned group. See [Group policies](<course-policies.rmd#sec-gp>) for details.
1. Late submissions are not accepted.
1. All submissions must be made in DataLab as a Jupyter notebook (`.ipynb`) and placed in the correct group workspace and the “submissions” folder. See [DataCamp policies](<course-policies.rmd#sec-dcp>).
1. At the deadline, missing, incorrect, empty, or corrupted files in the required DataLab location (correct group workspace and the “submissions” folder) will receive a zero for the group.
1. For $E_1$ and $E_2$, groups must answer 3 out of 4 questions; for $E_F$, groups must answer 4 out of 5 questions. Answering more than the required number of questions may result in a significant mark deduction.
1. In 3-hour class sessions, attendance for the post-exam review session (Zoom) is compulsory; students are required to log in during the review portion to register attendance.
1. The final exam $E_F$ is the last graded activity of the semester. No extra activities are planned after $E_F$ to increase marks or pass the course.
1. The final exam $E_F$ date may be adjusted for administrative reasons in the case of graduating students.
1. Students may lose the right to take their final exam due to excessive absences, according to current university regulations. See [Tardiness policies](<course-policies.rmd#sec-tp>) for details.
:::

<a id="sec-gp"></a>

::: {.callout-important}
### Groups policies.

1. Group size and formation: Groups may include up to four students. Group activities may be completed by one, two, three, or four students. Students form groups at the beginning of the semester.
1. Switching groups: A student may switch groups only with written consent from both (a) the group they are leaving and (b) the group they are joining. This requires at least two emails, one from each group, and each email must CC all members of both groups and me. I must authorize the change to confirm that no group exceeds four members. Requests must be submitted no later than one week before the next exam or the final exam. Requests received after this deadline will be rejected.
1. Expelling a member: Expulsion is an available option for all teams. To proceed, the team must provide a previously signed team agreement that defines expectations (e.g., communication, deadlines, contribution standards) and evidence showing the member did not meet them. Without a signed agreement and clear evidence, I cannot authorize the expulsion.
1. Unauthorized changes are not allowed: Students may not change groups or expel a member on their own. Any group change or expulsion is official only after it follows the policies above and receives my authorization. Until then, the original group membership remains in effect for all graded activities.
1. Team agreements: I strongly advise each team to create written rules and expectations at the start of the semester (e.g., communication norms, task division, deadlines, and how contributions will be documented). These agreements are managed within the team; I do not intervene in internal team arrangements. Because coevaluation has a significant impact on individual marks in group work, documenting contributions and expectations from the start is essential.
:::

<a id="sec-tp"></a>

::: {.callout-important}
### Tardiness policies.

1. Face-to-face tardiness: In face-to-face classes, two tardies count as one absence. Tardies accumulate over the course of the semester. A tardy is recorded if the student arrives immediately after attendance is taken and remains for the rest of the class. If a student is marked as present or tardy, this can be changed to an absence if they leave the classroom or do not stay until the end of the class.
1. Online attendance: In online classes, there is no tardiness policy. Attendance is based on the Zoom activity log (minutes connected). Each session results in either an attendance or an absence. Students must be connected for at least 90% of the class to be marked present. You must join using your own Zoom login credentials to verify identity and correctly attribute attendance.
1. Online connectivity: In online classes, connectivity problems do not excuse absences. Attendance is determined solely by the Zoom activity log (time connected). Students are responsible for ensuring reliable access and joining with their own Zoom account.
1. Zoom sessions in a face-to-face course: In face-to-face classes, we usually hold a few Zoom sessions during the semester (see @sec-schedule for details). During these sessions, the policy is the same as in online classes.
1. Weather-related emergency switch to Zoom: In face-to-face classes, it is possible that an occasional session may be moved to Zoom due to extreme weather conditions that cannot be reasonably anticipated. If this happens, I will notify students by email with updated instructions.
1. One-time Zoom exception for face-to-face sessions: In face-to-face classes, students may attend a designated face-to-face session via Zoom once per semester as an exception. If this applies to you, notify me by email. If it happens a second time, the student may still attend via Zoom, but I will record an absence in accordance with current university regulations.
1. Maximum absences: With respect to the maximum absences policy, we will adhere to the current university regulations.
:::

<a id="sec-dcp"></a>

::: {.callout-important}
### DataCamp policies.

*This section is the official submission policy for all graded activities (homework and exams). If any other section summarizes these rules, this section prevails.*

1. Platform and backup: We rely on DataLab (DataCamp) for completing homework assignments and exams (see @sec-activities). DataLab is the official course workspace: you will work there, and I will access your work there to evaluate it. In this course, there is typically no separate “upload-and-submit” step; instead, you are responsible for ensuring your work is saved and located correctly in DataLab for grading. Because these platforms may experience occasional downtime, you must have RStudio installed on your local computer as a backup so you can continue working in an emergency.
1. Required format and environment: All homework assignments and exams must be completed and made available for grading within DataLab as Jupyter notebooks (<tt>`.ipynb`</tt>). Although DataLab may allow uploads in formats such as PDF or MS Word, those formats are not acceptable for evaluated homework or exams in this course. You must complete your work directly in the provided Jupyter notebook environment to ensure consistency and proper evaluation.
1. “Submissions” folder requirement: In DataLab, students often create multiple documents and folders. For convenience, I have created a folder called “submissions” in your group workspace. You must place the notebooks I need to evaluate (assignments and exams) in this folder. Do not rename the folder.
1. Correct location is required: Because DataLab and Jupyter notebooks may be new tools for some students, you must be careful when providing work for grading. All activities must be placed in the correct group workspace (identified by the group name) and inside the “submissions” folder. Work placed in the wrong workspace or folder—even if completed—will be treated as not submitted. No exceptions will be made for work located in incorrect folders or workspaces.
1. After completing your final exam $E_F$, you will no longer have editor access to DataLab. Therefore, I suggest that you create a backup of any documents you wish to keep.
:::
